questions.question_text: A seller is preparing to list a single-family home in Portland. Under Maine law, when must the Property Disclosure Statement be provided?
questions.correct_answer: Before or at time of offer
distractors: Only after an accepted purchase offer
questions.explanation: Maine Title 33, Sections 171-176 requires sellers of 1-4 unit residential property to provide a written Property Disclosure Statement to prospective buyers before or at the time an offer is made. This gives buyers material information to make an informed decision.

questions.question_text: Which property type triggers the Maine seller disclosure requirement?
questions.explanation: Maine's Property Disclosure Statement requirement applies to sellers of 1-4 unit residential property under Title 33, Sections 171-176. Commercial properties, vacant land without residential structures, and properties with more than 4 units are not covered by this specific requirement.

questions.question_text: A seller fills out the Maine Property Disclosure Statement. Which item must be disclosed?
distractors: Prior listing price and sale history
questions.explanation: Maine's disclosure form requires sellers to disclose known defects along with information about water supply, access, flood zone status, heating, and insulation. The form addresses the physical condition of the property, not neighborhood disputes or financial projections.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives the seller's Property Disclosure Statement and discovers the roof leaks. The seller marked 'unknown' for that item. What is the seller's legal obligation?
questions.explanation: Maine law requires sellers to disclose known material defects honestly. If a seller knows the roof leaks but marks 'unknown,' they have violated the disclosure requirement. The duty is to disclose what is known, not to guarantee condition or make repairs.

questions.question_text: Which items must a Maine seller address on the Property Disclosure Statement?
questions.explanation: Maine's Property Disclosure Statement requires information about water supply, property access, flood zone status, known defects, heating systems, and insulation. It focuses on the physical characteristics and condition of the property rather than neighborhood or municipal data.

questions.question_text: A rural Maine home uses a private well. On the disclosure, which water supply detail must the seller provide?
questions.explanation: Maine's disclosure requires sellers to provide water supply information including type, location, any malfunctions, the most recent test date, and any unsatisfactory results. Given Maine's many rural properties with private wells, water supply disclosure is particularly important.

questions.question_text: A seller's private well had unsatisfactory test results two years ago. What must the seller disclose?
distractors: Only the most recent passing test
Nothing if the issue was resolved
Only results from the past year
questions.explanation: Maine disclosure law requires sellers to report unsatisfactory water test results. Even if retesting showed improvement, prior unsatisfactory results are material facts that must be disclosed so buyers can make informed decisions about the water supply.

questions.question_text: What heating system information must a Maine seller disclose?
questions.explanation: Maine's Property Disclosure Statement requires sellers to report the type of heating system, its current condition, and any known problems. The form does not require cost estimates, brand details, or contractor records, though sellers may voluntarily share such information.

questions.question_text: A home in rural Maine has a septic system that backs up occasionally. How should the seller handle this on the disclosure?
questions.correct_answer: Report the system type and issues
distractors: Omit it if repairs are planned
questions.explanation: Maine's disclosure requires sellers to identify the waste disposal system type (septic or sewer), its condition, and any known issues. A septic system that backs up occasionally is a known issue that must be disclosed honestly, regardless of planned repairs.

questions.question_text: Why does Maine place particular emphasis on water supply disclosures for residential property?
questions.correct_answer: Many rural properties use private wells
distractors: State law bans all municipal water systems
questions.explanation: Maine's emphasis on water supply disclosure stems from its large number of rural properties that rely on private wells rather than municipal water systems. Unlike public water which is regularly tested by municipalities, private well water quality is the homeowner's responsibility to monitor.

questions.question_text: A home built in 1965 is being sold in Maine. Which hazardous material must the seller address on the disclosure?
questions.explanation: Maine's disclosure requires sellers to address hazardous materials including lead paint, asbestos, radon, and underground storage tanks. For homes built before 1978, lead paint is a particularly relevant concern and must be disclosed under both Maine and federal law.

questions.question_text: Which hazardous substances must Maine sellers disclose on the Property Disclosure Statement?
questions.explanation: Maine's disclosure specifically addresses lead paint, asbestos, radon, and underground storage tanks. These four categories represent the most common residential hazardous material concerns in Maine properties and must be disclosed if known to the seller.

questions.question_text: A bank sells a foreclosed home in Bangor. Must the bank provide a Property Disclosure Statement?
distractors: Only if the home is occupied
questions.explanation: Court-ordered transfers including foreclosures are exempt from Maine's Property Disclosure Statement requirement. Other exemptions include estate sales, bankruptcy transfers, eminent domain, government transfers, living trust transfers, and deeds correcting prior deeds with no change in consideration.

questions.question_text: A property is transferred into a family living trust. Is a disclosure statement required?
questions.correct_answer: No, living trust transfers are exempt
distractors: Only if a minor is involved
Only if the trust is revocable
questions.explanation: Living trust transfers are among the exemptions to Maine's Property Disclosure Statement requirement. Other exempt transfers include court-ordered sales (estate, foreclosure, bankruptcy, eminent domain), government transfers, and corrective deeds with no change in consideration.

questions.question_text: A seller signs a corrective deed fixing a legal description error with no change in price. Is a new disclosure required?
distractors: Only if recorded in a new county
Only if over 90 days have passed
questions.explanation: Deeds that correct or modify a prior deed with no change in consideration are exempt from Maine's disclosure requirement. The exemption recognizes that corrective deeds are administrative in nature and do not represent a new sale requiring fresh disclosure.
